subtopics.title: Components
subtopics.content:
#### Boards
- **1 main game board** — Map of the Alps showing mountain basins, rivers flowing downhill, dam positions, conduit routes, and powerhouse locations.
- **4 company boards** — Player boards tracking construction resources (excavators, concrete mixers), technology tiles, and executive officers.
- **1 energy track** — Tracks energy produced by each player and determines turn order.
- **1 management board** — Wheel mechanism for recycling construction machinery.

#### Construction Pieces (Per Player)
- **Dams** — Base dams (level 1), elevation pieces (level 2, 3). Placed in basins to hold water.
- **Conduits** — Connect basins to powerhouses. Route water for power generation.
- **Powerhouses** — Generate energy when water flows through. Different capacities.

#### Other Components
- **Water drops** — Blue tokens representing water in the system.
- **Excavators and concrete mixers** — Construction machinery needed to build. Spent when building and slowly recycled.
- **Technology tiles** — Upgrades that improve building efficiency or provide special abilities.
- **Contract tiles** — Fulfill for VP and bonuses.
- **Executive officer tiles** — Asymmetric leader abilities for each company.
- **Round tokens** — Mark the 5 rounds.
- **VP tokens** — Track victory points.

subtopics.title: Board Setup
subtopics.content:
#### Main Board
1. **Place the map board** — The Alps map shows interconnected basins at different elevations.
2. **Water placement** — Place starting water drops in headwater basins as indicated.
3. **Neutral dams** — Place neutral dam pieces on designated starting positions.
4. **Contract tiles** — Shuffle and deal contract tiles into the display slots.
5. **Technology tiles** — Sort technology tiles and place in their supply.
6. **Management wheel** — Set the construction wheel to its starting position.

#### Round Setup
- **5 rounds** — Place round markers. Each round has specific water replenishment.
- **Headwater sources** — At the start of each round, new water drops are added to headwater basins.
- **Turn order** — Determined by position on the energy track (lowest energy goes first).

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content:
#### Company Board Setup
- **Choose a company** — Each player takes a company board with starting resources.
- **Starting machinery** — Each player begins with a set number of excavators and concrete mixers.
- **Executive officer** — Each player selects (or is dealt) an executive officer providing a unique power.
- **Starting structures** — Players may have a starting dam or conduit based on company.
- **Credits** — Each player starts with a small number of credits (money).

#### Construction Wheel
- **Machinery recycling** — The construction wheel slowly returns spent machinery. Built structures cost machinery that enters the wheel and comes back in 2-3 rounds.
- **Planning ahead** — You must plan construction knowing machinery won't be immediately available again.

subtopics.title: Game Overview
subtopics.content:
#### Core Concept
Barrage is a deeply strategic game about building a hydroelectric empire in the Alps. Players represent energy companies competing to build dams, conduits, and powerhouses to generate the most electricity. The signature mechanic is that water flows downhill — after you use water to generate power, it flows into a lower basin where an opponent's dam may capture it. This creates intense spatial competition and timing decisions.

#### Game Arc
- **5 rounds** — Short, brutal rounds with extremely tight action economy.
- **Construction machinery** — Your excavators and concrete mixers are spent when building and recycle slowly via the construction wheel. Running out of machinery means you cannot build.
- **Escalating infrastructure** — Early-game building creates the network that generates mid-to-late-game energy.
- **Water flow** — Water starts in mountain headwaters and flows down through the system, potentially powering multiple players' infrastructure.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
#### Round Structure
1. **Water replenishment** — Add new water drops to headwater basins as shown on the round marker.
2. **Income** — Players receive income (credits) based on their current position.
3. **Action phase** — Starting with the player lowest on the energy track, players take turns placing workers on action spaces. Each player has a limited number of engineers (workers).
4. **Water flow resolution** — After all actions, water flows downhill from any basins that produced energy.
5. **Round scoring** — Check for contract fulfillment and bonus VP.
6. **Construction wheel advance** — Rotate the wheel one notch, returning some spent machinery to players.

#### Action Spaces
- **Construction spaces** — Build dams, conduits, or powerhouses by spending machinery and credits.
- **Technology spaces** — Acquire technology tiles to improve efficiency.
- **Contract spaces** — Take contract tiles or fulfill existing ones.
- **Water management** — Manipulate water placement or flow.
- **External works** — Produce energy using special external contract mechanisms.
- **Turbine room** — Place a turbine to activate a dam-conduit-powerhouse network and produce energy.

#### Engineer Placement
- **Limited workers** — Each player has only 12 engineers total (not all available each round).
- **Blocking** — Most action spaces allow only 1 player per round.
- **Turn order advantage** — Going first (lowest on energy track) lets you claim key spaces.

subtopics.title: Key Actions
subtopics.content:
#### Building Infrastructure
- **Dams** — Placed in basins at specific elevations. Hold water drops. Can be elevated (level 1 → 2 → 3) to hold more water. Higher dams hold more but cost more machinery.
- **Conduits** — Connect a basin (with your dam) to a powerhouse. The longer the conduit, the more energy generated but the more it costs.
- **Powerhouses** — Receive water from conduits and convert it to energy. Each has a capacity (number of water drops it can process per activation).

#### Producing Energy
- **Activation** — Use the Turbine Room action to activate one of your production networks: dam → conduit → powerhouse.
- **Energy output** — Energy produced = number of water drops × conduit multiplier.
- **Water flow** — After production, the water drops flow downhill to the next lower basin. If an opponent has a dam there, they now hold that water.

#### Construction Costs
- **Excavators** — Required for conduits (1 per section) and dams.
- **Concrete mixers** — Required for dams and powerhouses.
- **Credits** — Additional monetary cost for most structures.
- **Machinery recycling** — Spent machinery enters the construction wheel and returns in 2-3 rounds. This is the core constraint of the game.

#### Technology Tiles
- **Efficiency upgrades** — Reduce construction costs or increase energy output.
- **Special abilities** — New action options or modified rules for your company.
- **Limited supply** — Some technologies are one-per-player. First come, first served.

#### Contracts
- **Requirements** — Produce a certain amount of energy in a single activation.
- **Rewards** — VP and immediate bonuses (credits, machinery, VP).
- **Fulfillment timing** — You must produce the required energy in one activation to fulfill a contract.

subtopics.title: Special Rules
subtopics.content:
#### Water Flow
- **Downhill movement** — After energy production, water drops move from the powerhouse exit to the next lower basin on the river.
- **Opponent capture** — If an opponent has a dam in that lower basin, they now control the water.
- **Multi-use water** — A single water drop can generate energy for multiple players as it flows down the mountain.
- **Strategic positioning** — Building below opponents is powerful because you get their used water for free.

#### Construction Wheel
- **Recycling mechanism** — The wheel has multiple slots. Spent machinery enters the wheel at the current position and comes out 2-3 rounds later.
- **Planning constraint** — You cannot build if you don't have the required machinery. Planning ahead for machinery availability is essential.
- **Tight economy** — This is the primary constraint. Running out of excavators or mixers freezes your construction.

#### Executive Officers
- **Asymmetric powers** — Each officer provides a unique game-changing ability.
- **Examples** — Extra machinery return, bonus energy on activation, cheaper construction, additional workers.
- **Strategic alignment** — Choose an officer that complements your positional strategy on the map.

#### External Works
- **Alternative energy** — Some action spaces let you produce energy without your own dam-conduit-powerhouse network.
- **Contracts** — External works can fulfill contracts.
- **Opportunity cost** — Using external works means not building your own infrastructure.

subtopics.title: Scoring and Endgame
subtopics.content:
#### Game End
- **After round 5** — The game ends after the 5th round.

#### Final Scoring
- **Energy track VP** — Points based on your position on the energy track.
- **Contract VP** — Points from fulfilled contracts throughout the game.
- **Objective tiles** — End-game objectives revealed at the start (score for specific achievements like most dams, highest elevation structures, etc.).
- **Building bonuses** — Some structures and technologies provide end-game VP.
- **Credits** — Remaining credits may be worth VP (at a poor conversion rate).

#### Winner
- **Highest VP** — Player with the most victory points wins.
- **Tiebreaker** — Player higher on the energy track.

subtopics.title: Beginner Tips
subtopics.content:
- **Machinery is everything** — You will constantly feel short on excavators and concrete mixers. Plan 2 rounds ahead for what you need.
- **Build below opponents** — Positioning your dams below an opponent's powerhouse exit gives you free water.
- **Don't overbuild early** — Each structure costs machinery that takes rounds to return. Build only what you can activate.
- **Contracts drive VP** — Raw energy production matters less than contract fulfillment. Always have a contract to work toward.
- **Turn order is tactical** — Being lower on the energy track lets you act first. Sometimes producing less energy is strategically better.
- **Technology matters** — A well-timed technology tile can save you machinery over multiple rounds.

subtopics.title: Key Strategies
subtopics.content:
#### Strategy 1: Downstream Parasitism
- **Goal** — Build infrastructure below opponents to capture their used water.
- **Key actions** — Position dams in lower basins, build conduits to your powerhouses.
- **Benefit** — Free water every time opponents produce energy.

#### Strategy 2: High Elevation Monopoly
- **Goal** — Control headwater basins with tall dams to hold maximum water.
- **Key actions** — Rush to build and elevate dams in mountain basins.
- **Benefit** — You control when water enters the system and can time activations for maximum contract value.

#### Strategy 3: Technology Rush
- **Goal** — Acquire key technology tiles to reduce costs and increase efficiency.
- **Key actions** — Prioritize technology action spaces early.
- **Benefit** — Lower construction costs compound over the entire game.

#### General Principles
- **Every action is precious** — With only ~12 actions per round and 5 rounds, there is zero room for wasted turns.
- **Watch the wheel** — Know exactly when your machinery returns and plan construction accordingly.
- **Block strategically** — Preventing an opponent from building in a key location can be worth more than your own construction.
